Article.ArticleTitle: Factors influencing antimicrobial decision-making in respiratory tract infections: A qualitative interview study with physicians and antimicrobial stewardship pharmacists.

Abstract.AbstractText: Respiratory tract infections (RTIs) have one of the highest rates of inappropriate antimicrobial use, which contributes to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and poor patient outcomes. This study aimed to describe factors that influence physicians and antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) pharmacists' decision-making around antimicrobials prescribed in hospitalised patients with RTIs.

Semi-structured interviews were conducted with physicians and AMS pharmacists from hospitals in New South Wales, Australia. Interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and coded for inductive thematic analysis.

Seventeen participants (ten AMS pharmacists and seven physicians) were interviewed. Five themes influencing antimicrobial decision-making were identified: considerations in the diagnosis of RTIs, harm to the patient and the physician, clinical experience and knowledge, ease of using an antimicrobial, and desire to minimise AMR. Diagnoses of RTIs were reported to be at times difficult and focused on speculating if symptoms were infective and what the likely pathogen was. Consideration was given to the likely patient harm based on the infection, medical history, and comorbidities, as well as the feasibility of antimicrobial administration and resistance. Clinical experience influenced these considerations and decisions. There was a desire amongst healthcare professionals to minimise antimicrobial use and prescribe optimally.

Concerns for patient deterioration, diagnostic difficulty in RTIs and uncertainty, lack of AMS knowledge and training, and administration constraints challenged optimal prescribing of antimicrobials. Improving equity of access to diagnostics, ensuring proper AMS training for healthcare professionals, and finding solutions to administration issues with narrow spectrum antimicrobials will assist in optimising antimicrobial prescribing and potentially mitigating AMR.
